channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Disposables Banned, Flavors Next: Irish Vape Crackdown Gathers Pace
item.description: Remove the products adults actually use to stay off cigarettes, and the outcome may not be the one policymakers expect. Ireland’s proposed restrictions on disposable vapes and flavours risk making vaping less accessible, less appealing and less useful for people trying to quit smoking. Protecting young people matters, but so does avoiding a policy that pushes adults back towards cigarettes or into informal markets. Good regulation should reduce harm in practice, not just look tough on paper. (Published on July 15, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: Tobacco Harm Reduction for Older Smokers
item.description: Tobacco harm reduction could benefit older adults struggling to quit smoking, as they often face deep nicotine dependence. Traditional cessation methods are often ineffective for long-term smokers. Tobacco harm reduction offers diverse alternatives, like e-cigarettes and nicotine pouches, that better address nicotine dependence. Research shows older smokers may lack knowledge about these alternatives. Encouraging accurate information and exploring harm reduction options can support older adults in making the switch and overcoming barriers to quitting. Involving their community can provide essential support. Embracing harm reduction offers a realistic path forward for older smokers, even after years of tobacco use. (Published on July 15, 2026bytobaccoharmreduction.net)

item.title: The Quiet Revolution in New Zealand’s War on Smoking
item.description: New Zealand has successfully reduced smoking rates through innovative tobacco control measures, including regulating access to vaping products. This approach, endorsed by health authorities, led to a rapid decline in smoking rates, especially among indigenous communities and youth. The country's experience shows that harm reduction policies can complement traditional tobacco control efforts and accelerate progress towards a smoke-free future. The success of these measures highlights the importance of evidence-based regulation and a focus on reducing tobacco-related harms, particularly among disadvantaged populations. New Zealand's approach serves as a valuable example of how innovation can drive positive change in public health. (Published on July 15, 2026byweareinnovation.global)

item.title: Philippine Authorities Pushing for Tougher Penalties in Illicit Tobacco Fight
item.description: Lawmakers and prosecutors in the Philippines are urging stronger efforts to combat the rising illicit tobacco trade due to ineffective seizures. They emphasize the need for convictions to deter criminal networks in the lucrative trade. A recent study estimated a significant tax revenue loss of ₱141 billion ($2.3 billion) over two years. Proposed actions include an Anti-Illicit Trade law to enhance prosecutions, better coordination, and the use of track-and-trace technology. Prosecutors highlight the need for additional evidence in ongoing cases. (Published on July 15, 2026bytobaccoreporter.com)

item.title: Bridging the Epidemiological Evidence From Snus to Nicotine Pouches | #GFN26ScienceLab
item.description: Snus changed what we know about nicotine, smoke and disease risk. Decades of epidemiological evidence show that removing combustion can dramatically change the health profile of nicotine use — a lesson now highly relevant to nicotine pouches. The question is how carefully that evidence can be applied to newer smoke-free products without ignoring important differences between categories. Better policy starts with understanding what long-term data can tell us, and where new evidence is still needed. (Published on July 15, 2026bygfn.tv)
